tree.title: Peptides: The Molecular Alphabet
tree.summary: This tutorial explains how amino acids serve as building blocks that connect through peptide bonds to form proteins with multiple structural levels.

children.title: 1. The Building Blocks: Amino Acids
children.summary: Amino acids are the 20 fundamental organic building blocks that combine in different sequences to form over 100,000 different proteins in the human body, and are classified into four types (nonpolar, polar, acidic, and basic) based on their side chain properties that determine how they interact with water and other molecules.

children.title: Introduction to Amino Acids as Building Blocks
children.summary: Amino acids are organic molecules that serve as the fundamental building blocks of proteins, with 20 standard types creating over 100,000 different proteins.
children.sourceText:
What Are Amino Acids? Amino acids are organic molecules that serve as the fundamental building blocks of proteins. There are 20 standard amino acids encoded by DNA, and despite this small alphabet, they combine to create over 100,000 different proteins in the human body. 
Think of amino acids like letters in an alphabet. Just as 26 letters can form hundreds of thousands of English words, 20 amino acids can form virtually unlimited protein 'words.' The sequence matters enormously — just as 'STOP' and 'POTS' use the same letters but mean different things, the same amino acids in different orders create completely different proteins.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: What Are Amino Acids? Amino acids are organic molecules that serve as the fundamental building blocks of proteins.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: the same amino acids in different orders create completely different proteins.

children.title: Classification of Amino Acids by Side Chain Properties
children.summary: Amino acids are classified into four types based on their side chain properties, which determine how they interact with water and other molecules.
children.sourceText: The Four Types of Amino Acids Amino acids are classified by their side chain (R group) properties, which determine how they interact with water and other molecules:

sourceAnchor.startMarker: The Four Types of Amino Acids Amino acids are classified by their side chain (R group) properties
sourceAnchor.endMarker: which determine how they interact with water and other molecules:

children.title: Nonpolar Amino Acids Properties and Behavior
children.summary: Nonpolar amino acids have hydrocarbon side chains that avoid water and cluster together in protein interiors.
children.sourceText: Nonpolar amino acids have hydrocarbon side chains that don't interact well with water. In proteins, they tend to cluster together in the interior, away from the aqueous environment — like oil droplets avoiding water.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Nonpolar amino acids have hydrocarbon side chains that don't interact well with water.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: like oil droplets avoiding water.

children.title: Polar Amino Acids Properties and Location
children.summary: Polar amino acids can form hydrogen bonds with water and are typically found on protein surfaces to enhance solubility.
children.sourceText: Polar amino acids have side chains that can form hydrogen bonds with water. They're often found on protein surfaces, making proteins soluble.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Polar amino acids have side chains that can form hydrogen bonds with water.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: They're often found on protein surfaces, making proteins soluble.

children.title: Charged Amino Acids and Their Interactions
children.summary: Charged amino acids carry electrical charge at physiological pH and are crucial for enzyme function and protein interactions through ionic bonds.
children.sourceText: Charged amino acids carry electrical charge at physiological pH (7.4). They form ionic bonds and are crucial for enzyme active sites and protein-protein interactions.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Charged amino acids carry electrical charge at physiological pH (7.4).
sourceAnchor.endMarker: They form ionic bonds and are crucial for enzyme active sites and protein-protein interactions.

children.title: Memory Trick for Charged Amino Acids
children.summary: A helpful memory device categorizes acidic amino acids as 'negative nancys' and basic amino acids as 'positive pals' that interact through salt bridges.
children.sourceText: Memory trick: Acidic amino acids (D, E) are 'negative nancys' and basic amino acids (K, R, H) are 'positive pals.' The charged residues love to interact with each other through salt bridges!

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Memory trick: Acidic amino acids (D, E) are 'negative nancys' and basic amino acids (K, R, H) are 'positive pals.'
sourceAnchor.endMarker: The charged residues love to interact with each other through salt bridges!

children.title: 2. Amino Acid Structure
children.summary: All amino acids share a common structure with a central alpha carbon bonded to an amino group, carboxyl group, hydrogen, and variable side chain, existing as zwitterions at physiological pH, with life predominantly using the L-form of these 20 standard building blocks (plus rare exceptions like selenocysteine and pyrrolysine).

children.title: Universal Amino Acid Backbone Structure
children.summary: All amino acids share the same core structure with a central alpha carbon bonded to four different groups.
children.sourceText:
The Universal Backbone Every amino acid shares the same core structure — a central alpha carbon (Cα) bonded to four different groups: 

[EXAMPLE: Amino Acid General Structure]         R (side chain)
        |
   H₃N⁺—Cα—COO⁻
        |
        H

At physiological pH (7.4), amino acids exist as
zwitterions: positively charged amino group,
negatively charged carboxyl group.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: The Universal Backbone Every amino acid shares the same core structure
sourceAnchor.endMarker: negatively charged carboxyl group.

children.title: Chirality and L vs D Forms
children.summary: Amino acids (except glycine) are chiral molecules with left-handed and right-handed forms, with life predominantly using L-amino acids.
children.sourceText: Chirality matters! All amino acids (except glycine) are chiral — they have 'left-handed' (L) and 'right-handed' (D) forms. Life almost exclusively uses L-amino acids. D-amino acids are found in some bacterial cell walls and antibiotics.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Chirality matters! All amino acids (except glycine) are chiral
sourceAnchor.endMarker: found in some bacterial cell walls and antibiotics.

children.title: Why Only 20 Standard Amino Acids
children.summary: The genetic code uses 64 possible codons to specify 20 amino acids, making it degenerate with multiple codons for some amino acids.
children.sourceText: Why Only 20 Amino Acids?] DNA uses 3-nucleotide codons to specify amino acids. With 4 bases (A, T, G, C), there are 4³ = 64 possible codons. This is more than enough for 20 amino acids, so the genetic code is degenerate — multiple codons can specify the same amino acid.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Why Only 20 Amino Acids?] DNA uses 3-nucleotide codons
sourceAnchor.endMarker: multiple codons can specify the same amino acid.

children.title: Expanded Amino Acid Alphabet Beyond 20
children.summary: Some organisms and engineered systems can incorporate additional amino acids beyond the standard 20, including selenocysteine, pyrrolysine, and synthetic amino acids.
children.sourceText: Some organisms have expanded this alphabet. Selenocysteine (the 21st amino acid) and pyrrolysine (the 22nd) are incorporated via special mechanisms. Scientists have even engineered organisms to incorporate completely synthetic amino acids!

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Some organisms have expanded this alphabet. Selenocysteine
sourceAnchor.endMarker: incorporate completely synthetic amino acids!

children.title: 3. Peptide Bond Formation
children.summary: Peptide bonds form between amino acids through dehydration synthesis, where the carboxyl group of one amino acid reacts with the amino group of another to create a rigid C-N bond while releasing a water molecule.

children.title: Dehydration Synthesis Mechanism and Basic Reaction
children.summary: Peptide bonds form through dehydration synthesis when the carboxyl group of one amino acid reacts with the amino group of another, releasing water.
children.sourceText:
Dehydration Synthesis: Building the Chain When two amino acids join, they form a peptide bond through a dehydration synthesis (also called condensation) reaction. The carboxyl group of one amino acid reacts with the amino group of another, releasing a water molecule. 

[EXAMPLE: The Peptide Bond Reaction] Amino Acid 1      Amino Acid 2
    |                  |
  -COOH    +    H₂N-   →   -CO-NH-   +   H₂O
    ↑                ↑         ↑           ↑
Carboxyl        Amino      Peptide     Water
  group         group       bond      released

The C-N bond formed is called the peptide bond.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Dehydration Synthesis: Building the Chain When two amino acids join
sourceAnchor.endMarker: The C-N bond formed is called the peptide bond.

children.title: Peptide Bond Characteristics and Properties
children.summary: Peptide bonds have partial double-bond character making them planar and rigid, with remarkable properties due to resonance.
children.sourceText:
It has partial double-bond character (planar, rigid). Think of it like connecting Lego bricks. Each amino acid has a 'male' end (amino group) and a 'female' end (carboxyl group). When you snap them together, a small piece (water) pops off to make room for the connection. The chain always grows in one direction: N-terminus → C-terminus. 
[CALLOUT: In cells, peptide bonds are formed by ribosomes using ATP energy. The reaction is thermodynamically unfavorable, so it requires energy input. Breaking peptide bonds (hydrolysis) releases energy and is used by proteases to digest proteins. 
[DEEPDIVE: The Chemistry of Peptide Bonds] The peptide bond has some remarkable properties due to resonance:

sourceAnchor.startMarker: It has partial double-bond character (planar, rigid).
sourceAnchor.endMarker: The peptide bond has some remarkable properties due to resonance:

children.title: Lego Analogy for Peptide Bond Formation
children.summary: Peptide bond formation is like connecting Lego bricks, with amino acids having 'male' and 'female' ends that snap together while releasing water.
children.sourceText: Think of it like connecting Lego bricks. Each amino acid has a 'male' end (amino group) and a 'female' end (carboxyl group). When you snap them together, a small piece (water) pops off to make room for the connection. The chain always grows in one direction: N-terminus → C-terminus.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Think of it like connecting Lego bricks.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: The chain always grows in one direction: N-terminus → C-terminus.

children.title: Cellular Energy Requirements for Peptide Formation
children.summary: In cells, ribosomes form peptide bonds using ATP energy because the reaction is thermodynamically unfavorable, while breaking bonds releases energy.
children.sourceText: In cells, peptide bonds are formed by ribosomes using ATP energy. The reaction is thermodynamically unfavorable, so it requires energy input. Breaking peptide bonds (hydrolysis) releases energy and is used by proteases to digest proteins.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: In cells, peptide bonds are formed by ribosomes using ATP energy.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: Breaking peptide bonds (hydrolysis) releases energy and is used by proteases to digest proteins.

children.title: Water Molecule Release in Protein Synthesis
children.summary: Each peptide bond formation releases one water molecule, so a 100 amino acid protein releases 99 water molecules during synthesis.
children.sourceText: Each peptide bond releases one water molecule. So a protein with 100 amino acids released 99 water molecules during synthesis. This is why it's called 'dehydration' synthesis!

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Each peptide bond releases one water molecule.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: This is why it's called 'dehydration' synthesis!

children.title: 4. Protein Structure Levels
children.summary: Proteins have four hierarchical levels of structural organization: primary (amino acid sequence), secondary (local folding patterns like α-helices and β-sheets), tertiary (complete 3D shape of a single chain), and quaternary (assembly of multiple polypeptide chains into functional complexes).

children.title: Four Levels of Protein Structure Organization
children.summary: Proteins are organized into four hierarchical structural levels, each building upon the previous one.
children.sourceText:
Proteins have four levels of structural organization, each building on the previous: 


### Primary Structure: The Sequence The primary structure is simply the linear sequence of amino acids, read from N-terminus to C-terminus. It's determined directly by the gene sequence and written using single-letter or three-letter codes. 

[EXAMPLE: Primary Structure Notation] Single-letter: MVLSPADKTNVKAAWGK...
Three-letter:  Met-Val-Leu-Ser-Pro-Ala-Asp-Lys-Thr-Asn-Val-Lys-Ala-Ala-Trp-Gly-Lys...

This is the beginning of human hemoglobin alpha chain.
The sequence determines everything else about the protein.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Proteins have four levels of structural organization, each building on the previous:
sourceAnchor.endMarker: The sequence determines everything else about the protein.

children.title: Secondary Structure and Local Folding Patterns
children.summary: Secondary structures are regular, repeating patterns stabilized by hydrogen bonds between backbone atoms.
children.sourceText:
Secondary structures are regular, repeating patterns stabilized by hydrogen bonds between backbone atoms (not side chains). 

[DEEPDIVE: α-Helix vs β-Sheet: When Does Each Form?] The amino acid sequence determines which secondary structure forms:

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Secondary structures are regular, repeating patterns stabilized by hydrogen bonds between backbone atoms
sourceAnchor.endMarker: The amino acid sequence determines which secondary structure forms:

children.title: Tertiary Structure and Three-Dimensional Shape
children.summary: Tertiary structure represents the complete 3D shape of a single polypeptide chain formed by side chain interactions.
children.sourceText:
Tertiary structure is the complete three-dimensional shape of a single polypeptide chain. It results from interactions between side chains (R groups): 

[CALLOUT: Anfinsen's dogma: The amino acid sequence contains all the information needed to fold into the correct 3D structure. Christian Anfinsen won the 1972 Nobel Prize for demonstrating this with ribonuclease A.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Tertiary structure is the complete three-dimensional shape of a single polypeptide chain.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: Christian Anfinsen won the 1972 Nobel Prize for demonstrating this with ribonuclease A.

children.title: Quaternary Structure and Multi-Chain Assembly
children.summary: Quaternary structure describes how multiple polypeptide chains assemble into functional protein complexes.
children.sourceText: Quaternary structure describes how multiple polypeptide chains (subunits) assemble into a functional complex. Not all proteins have quaternary structure — only those with multiple subunits.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Quaternary structure describes how multiple polypeptide chains (subunits) assemble into a functional complex.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: Not all proteins have quaternary structure — only those with multiple subunits.

children.title: House-Building Analogy for Protein Structure Levels
children.summary: The four protein structure levels are compared to building a house, from materials to complete structure.
children.sourceText: Think of the four structure levels like building a house: Primary = the list of building materials. Secondary = assembling materials into walls and beams (repeated patterns). Tertiary = arranging walls into a complete room (3D shape of one unit). Quaternary = combining rooms into a complete house (multiple units working together).

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Think of the four structure levels like building a house:
sourceAnchor.endMarker: Quaternary = combining rooms into a complete house (multiple units working together).

children.title: 5. Interactive Peptide Builder
children.summary: The Interactive Peptide Builder is a hands-on educational tool that allows users to construct peptide chains by selecting amino acids and observing the dehydration synthesis process, including peptide bond formation, water molecule release, and molecular structure visualization.

children.title: Interactive Peptide Builder Introduction
children.summary: Introduction to the interactive peptide builder tool and its main educational purpose.
children.sourceText: Interactive Demo: Use the Peptides app (/peptides) to build your own peptide chains! Select amino acids, watch dehydration synthesis release water molecules, and see how the chain grows from N-terminus to C-terminus.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Interactive Demo: Use the Peptides app (/peptides) to build
sourceAnchor.endMarker: chain grows from N-terminus to C-terminus.

children.title: Practice Activity with Famous Peptides
children.summary: Suggestion to practice using the builder with well-known peptide sequences.
children.sourceText: [DEEPDIVE: Try These Sequences!] Build these famous peptides in the interactive builder:

children.title: 6. Amazing Facts About Proteins
children.summary: This section explores fascinating protein facts, including titin as the largest known protein with over 34,000 amino acids, the mysterious speed of protein folding despite astronomical structural possibilities, and the serious health consequences when proteins fold incorrectly.

children.title: Titin: The Molecular Giant Spring
children.summary: Titin is the largest known protein, functioning as a molecular spring in muscles with extraordinary size and synthesis characteristics.
children.sourceText:
Titin is a molecular spring that gives muscles their elasticity. At 34,350 amino acids and ~3.8 million Daltons, it's so large that its gene name is the longest word in the English language (189,819 letters if you spelled it out chemically). 
Fun fact: Titin is so long that ribosomes take over 30 minutes to synthesize a single molecule. If stretched out, one titin molecule would be about 1 micrometer long — the size of a small bacterium!

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Titin is a molecular spring that gives muscles their elasticity.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: one titin molecule would be about 1 micrometer long — the size of a small bacterium!

children.title: The Protein Folding Problem and Levinthal's Paradox
children.summary: Despite astronomical possible conformations, proteins fold correctly in milliseconds by following energetically favorable pathways rather than random searching.
children.sourceText: The protein folding problem: How does a chain of amino acids find its correct 3D structure among astronomical possibilities? A 100-residue protein has roughly 10^100 possible conformations, yet folds in milliseconds. This paradox (Levinthal's paradox) shows that proteins don't search randomly — they follow energetically favorable folding pathways.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: The protein folding problem: How does a chain of amino acids find its correct 3D structure
sourceAnchor.endMarker: they follow energetically favorable folding pathways.

children.title: Protein Misfolding and Disease Consequences
children.summary: When proteins misfold incorrectly, it can lead to severe disease consequences.
children.sourceText: Protein Misfolding and Disease When proteins misfold, the consequences can be severe:

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Protein Misfolding and Disease When proteins misfold, the consequences can be severe:
sourceAnchor.endMarker: Protein Misfolding and Disease When proteins misfold, the consequences can be severe:

children.title: 7. Key Takeaways
children.summary: Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins, each containing an amino group, carboxyl group, alpha carbon, and unique side chain that link via peptide bonds to form chains that fold into functional 3D structures through four levels of organization.

children.title: Amino Acid Structure and Components
children.summary: Amino acids are protein building blocks with four key structural components including amino group, carboxyl group, alpha carbon, and unique side chains.
children.sourceText: Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins. Each has an amino group, carboxyl group, alpha carbon, and unique side chain.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins. Each has an amino group, carboxyl group, alpha carbon, and unique side chain.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins. Each has an amino group, carboxyl group, alpha carbon, and unique side chain.

children.title: Peptide Bond Formation Process
children.summary: Amino acids connect through peptide bonds formed by dehydration synthesis to create protein chains.
children.sourceText: They link via peptide bonds (dehydration synthesis), forming chains

sourceAnchor.startMarker: They link via peptide bonds (dehydration synthesis), forming chains
sourceAnchor.endMarker: They link via peptide bonds (dehydration synthesis), forming chains

children.title: Protein Folding and Organization
children.summary: Protein chains fold into functional three-dimensional structures through four distinct levels of structural organization.
children.sourceText: that fold into functional 3D structures through four levels of organization.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: that fold into functional 3D structures through four levels of organization.
